hypothesis: single-agent generalist baseline (PG 1.0810 SOTA stack)
notes: single-agent generalist / PG 1.0810 reference

hypothesis: Add per-head attention output sigmoid gate (init +5.0 → ~0.993 transparent at step 0) on all 11 layers, allowing the model to dampen redundant heads — a missing component vs the documented multi_596 reference at 1.072.
notes: Adds nn.Parameter attn_out_gate=[num_heads,] in CausalSelfAttention; applies sigmoid(gate) elementwise to y=[B,T,H,D] before reshape+proj. Total +88 fp32 params across 11 blocks (~352 bytes; passes through as fp16 in GPTQ pipeline). Mechanism: gated attention is a standard small-LM trick (cf. PR library #1721, #1632, #1692 etc). Init 5.0 chosen so step-0 forward is near-identity (sigmoid(5)≈0.993) and gradient signal is non-vanishing. Param classifies into scalar_params via ndim<2 check; not matched by CONTROL_TENSOR_NAME_PATTERNS so AdamW @ scalar_lr=0.02 with adam_wd=0.02. No size impact, no FA3 hazard, no torch.compile shape issue.

hypothesis: Freeze tok_emb during score-first TTT (tied embedding == output projection; per-chunk SGD on it overfits global vocab mapping to 32K-token windows).
notes: Excludes tok_emb.weight from ttt_params and sets requires_grad_(False) on it before TTT loop. Restores requires_grad_(True) on all params at end (existing code). Mechanism: tok_emb (~4M params, ~40% of total) is tied to logit projection, so per-chunk SGD shifts BOTH the input embedding and the output mapping. With 3 epochs * ~15 chunks * SGD-momentum-0.9, this accumulates noisy chunk-local-frequency bias. Freezing it keeps global vocab semantics stable while letting block matrices + scalar control tensors do per-chunk adaptation. Bonus: less work in optimizer.step / allreduce → small eval-budget reclaim, mitigating the eval_budget_overrun seen on exp_001.

hypothesis: Skip standalone quantized_sliding_window eval when TTT is enabled (TTT itself does sliding-window scoring; the standalone pass is purely diagnostic and redundant against a tight 600s budget) — combined with the freeze-tok_emb TTT change from exp_002.
notes: exp_002 produced val_bpb=1.080426 (improvement from baseline 1.081) but tipped over the eval budget by 3.8s due to running BOTH the standalone sliding-window pass AND the TTT pass. Per the run-rules note, primary metric regex matches quantized_ttt; sliding-window is fallback only when TTT is disabled. Gating the standalone pass with `not h.ttt_enabled` reclaims a full pass over val tokens at stride 64 (estimated 30-100s saved on 8 GPUs), more than enough headroom. The freeze-tok_emb mechanism from exp_002 is preserved. Expected: val_bpb≈1.0804 (within noise of exp_002), eval_s drops to ~520-570s, status=keep.

hypothesis: Raise TTT cosine LR floor from 0 to 0.4·ttt_lr so late val chunks (where the model has accumulated useful drift toward val distribution) keep adapting at meaningful LR instead of freezing.
notes: Score-first TTT iterates over ~305 chunks of 32K tokens with cosine LR decay over chunk index. Original schedule decays to ~0 LR at the last chunk so the latter half of val data sees minimal adaptation, despite (a) the model by then being much closer to val distribution (so gradient signal is cleaner) and (b) ~50s of eval-budget headroom exp_003 freed by skipping the standalone sliding-window pass. Mechanism: replace 0.5·(1+cos(πi/N)) with 0.4 + 0.6·0.5·(1+cos(πi/N)) — same shape, but late chunks get 0.4·ttt_lr instead of 0. Total integrated LR-time rises 1.5×, which slightly increases SGD-step cost via larger gradient norm (clipped at 1.0 already), so eval_s should rise <5s. Risk: cumulative drift could overshoot if late-chunk gradients reinforce a wrong direction; mitigated because chunks are sequential and gradients reflect local distribution. Expected delta: small but principled (-0.001).

hypothesis: Switch TTT inner SGD from heavy-ball to Nesterov-accelerated momentum (nesterov=True). Across ~58K inner steps spanning 305 chunks at constant per-chunk LR, the lookahead update should converge faster per step at zero memory/compute cost.
notes: SNOO (arxiv 2510.15830) finds that Nesterov on the inner-loop pseudo-gradient is the primary driver of DiLoCo-style gains over heavy-ball momentum; the same mechanism applies per-chunk in our score-first TTT (~192 inner steps × 305 chunks at constant LR). PyTorch SGD with nesterov=True requires momentum>0 and dampening==0 (both satisfied: momentum=0.9, dampening default 0). Score-first ordering and freeze_tok_emb preserved. Eval cost identical (same FLOPs, same buffer); only the update formula changes.

hypothesis: Layer-wise LR decay for TTT inner SGD: layer i gets 0.85^(10-i)·ttt_lr (5× total span across depth) — concentrate per-chunk adaptation on later blocks where distribution shift mostly manifests, keep early-layer token features stable. Outer cosine schedule, momentum, and step count unchanged.
notes: Discriminative fine-tuning principle (ULMFiT/BERT-style): lower layers handle general features, upper layers task-specific. With score-first TTT on 32K-token chunks, late layers carry the per-chunk drift — full LR concentrates capacity there, while 0.20× LR at layer 0 reduces overfit-to-chunk risk on the embedding-near layers. Compute-free (multiple param groups in same SGD). Companion to exp_004 (LR floor); orthogonal axis to exp_005 (Nesterov).

hypothesis: Add weight_decay=1e-4 to TTT SGD optimizer to mildly regularize cumulative drift across ~58K inner steps over 305 chunks; lr×wd=5e-7 per step gives ~3% pull-toward-zero washing out idiosyncratic per-chunk gradient noise while leaving persistent val-distribution signal intact.
notes: Recent TTT thread: exp_004 (cosine LR floor 0.4) KEEP -0.0004 implies persistent late-chunk adaptation helps; exp_006 (layer-wise LR decay 0.85) DISCARD +0.00057 implies all layers want full LR. Conclusion: TTT works well; cumulative drift is the under-explored failure mode. WD=1e-4 is the smallest structural addition (zero→nonzero) that addresses cumulative drift without dampening per-step adaptation. Mechanism: with no decoupling, momentum-driven weight excursions accumulate across 305 chunks; per-head scalars (q_gain, attn_out_gate at ~+5) and matrix weights all drift unboundedly. WD pulls toward zero — technically wrong anchor (would prefer pull-toward-init), but at 1e-4 the cumulative effect (~0.029×) is dominated by recent steps via momentum coupling, so de-facto it acts as a moving-average regularizer favoring chunks whose grads align persistently. Risk: scalar params (q_gain≈5.25) drift to ~5.10 (sigmoid 0.993→0.992; negligible); matrix weights at ±0.05 drift to ±0.0485 (mild capacity reg). If WD too aggressive: regression similar in scale to exp_006 (+0.0005). If just right: ~-0.001. Information-theoretically informative either way: tells us whether cumulative TTT drift is a real failure mode.

hypothesis: Sharper GPTQ calibration: Hessian damp factor 0.01→0.005 and lazy-batch block_size 128→32 — together push the post-quant weights closer to the FP optimum since both knobs trade calibration robustness for fidelity, an underexplored surface in this lineage (TTT-tweaks 005/006/007 all discarded).
notes: Untouched surface (quantization) vs crowded TTT direction. Damp 0.005 has external prior (PR #1318, ICLR 2026 "Geometry of LLM Quantization"). block_size=32 also realigns with the seed-stack documentation (system prompt explicitly says "block_size=32"). No size impact (purely calibration-time changes, output bytes identical). No budget impact (32-col Cholesky cheaper than 128-col; matmul flops the same total). Risk: aggressive calibration could slightly overfit to calibration set; mitigated by 64 calibration batches and per-row symmetric quant. If keep, follow-up: damp 0.005→0.003. If discard or marginal, pivot away from quantization.

hypothesis: Add PaLM-style z-loss aux (coef 1e-4 · mean(logsumexp(logits)^2)) to training/TTT cross-entropy: bounds the softmax partition function, tightening logit dynamic range so downstream GPTQ int6 quantization noise is relatively smaller per-token; orthogonal to the saturated TTT-tuning thread.
notes: Untouched loss-shape surface; PaLM/ST-MoE/GLM standard coefficient. Coefficient 1e-4 with logits already softcapped at 30 yields aux term ~0.5-1% of CE — non-disruptive but persistent regularization across the full ~600s train. Should help GPTQ post-quant especially since softmax is the binding metric. Same z-loss applies during TTT inner-SGD but at this coef won't meaningfully bias the per-chunk adaptation. Risk: tiny extra logsumexp compute in fullgraph compile — verified syntax+size locally; logsumexp is a standard reduction so compile should not break. Expect modest -0.0008 or null result; training-time only so eval budget unchanged.

hypothesis: Raise EMA_DECAY from 0.9965 to 0.999 (3.5× longer half-life: ~198 → ~693 steps) so end-of-training EMA averages a substantive slice of the 72% linear warmdown phase rather than only its tail — extracts more noise-reduction across late low-LR fine-tuning.
notes: Untouched surface (EMA averaging) — recent thread of 5 consecutive failures on TTT-inner-loop knobs (Nesterov, layer-LR, WD), GPTQ damp/block, and z-loss aux suggests TTT/loss surfaces are saturated. EMA half-life check: 0.9965 → ln(2)/0.0035=198 steps; 0.999 → ln(2)/0.001=693 steps. With ~10k total training steps and warmdown_frac=0.72 (so ~7200 steps in linearly-decaying LR phase), 0.9965 averages over ~3% of training (~tail of warmdown), while 0.999 averages over ~10% of training (~late-half of warmdown). Worst-case dilution risk is bounded since the LR is monotonically decreasing during warmdown, so older averaged steps are lower-quality but not random. Standard nanoGPT default for cosine-schedule pretraining. Single-line change, no compute or size impact.

hypothesis: Reset SGD momentum buffer at each TTT chunk boundary (optimizer.state.clear() per chunk). The single optimizer instance carries momentum across all 305 heterogeneous val-text chunks, so each chunk inherits stale ~10-step-filtered gradient state from prior chunks' (different-topic) tokens — clean per-chunk local momentum should give a more accurate descent direction while preserving the cumulative param drift exp_004 confirmed helps.
notes: Untouched surface: prior TTT failures (exp_005 Nesterov, exp_006 layer-LR decay, exp_007 WD) all touched per-step update rule; none touched cross-chunk optimizer state lifecycle. Mechanism: across heterogeneous val text, chunk-i gradient direction is local; momentum carrying chunk-(i-k) info adds stale-direction noise. Reset preserves param drift (which exp_004 showed beneficial) but cleans velocity buffer. Implementation: optimizer.state.clear() inserted after LR set, before epoch loop. Cheap (no extra compute), safe (SGD's only state is momentum_buffer; first step in chunk just becomes pure -lr*grad).

hypothesis: Freeze attention Q/K/V projections (c_q, c_k, c_v) during TTT in addition to tok_emb — the routing matrices shouldn't drift on chunk-local 32K windows; let attn.proj+MLP+norms absorb per-chunk feature-space adaptation (TENT/FFN-adaptation thesis, same structural-freeze axis as the historically strongest exp_002 move).
notes: Untouched surface: exp_005-011 all touched per-step TTT update rules (Nesterov, layer-LR, WD, GPTQ damp, z-loss, EMA decay, momentum reset) and all discarded. The "which params adapt" surface is exp_002's surface, where tok_emb-freeze gave the strongest historical signal (1.080426 < baseline 1.081 even with eval overrun). Q/K/V routing is the next brittlest component to a per-chunk SGD update because it controls attention pattern formation. Output proj (attn.proj) and MLP can still adapt to feature shift. Risk: gradient signal lost from QKV may slow adaptation; counter-evidence is that tok_emb-freeze (much larger param fraction) helped. Side effect: ~12% backward FLOP reduction in TTT inner loop — net eval time should shrink slightly.

hypothesis: Split TTT SGD into two param groups by ndim — 1D per-channel gates (q_gain, attn_out_gate, attn_scale, mlp_scale) get 5× ttt_lr (0.025), 2D matrix params keep 1× (0.005). Mirrors Muon's 1D-vs-2D separation in main training; uplifts the otherwise-sluggish gate scalars whose grad norms are ~100× smaller than matrices.
notes: Untouched axis: exp_005-012 all kept uniform LR across params (Nesterov form, layer-LR decay, WD, momentum reset, freeze-QKV). exp_006 (per-layer decay 0.85^(10-i)) was per-LAYER and monotone DECREASE; this is per-PARAM-CLASS and monotone ≥1× (only uplifts gates). Cosine schedule still applies, with floor 0.4 — both groups follow same shape, scaled by their lr_mult.

hypothesis: Apply eval-time temperature scaling T=1.05 to TTT score-first logits only (TTT training stays T=1.0): post-GPTQ-INT6 + score-first TTT can leave the softmax over-sharpened on chunk-local peaks, mild flattening recovers NLL on hard mid-tail tokens.
notes: New surface vs all 9 prior children of exp_004 (which all touched TTT inner-loop optimizer form, loss aux, EMA, GPTQ damp). Single-coefficient but qualitatively new eval pipeline stage. If KEEP, next iteration → forward-adaptive per-chunk T from a small grid (calibrated on chunk i, applied to chunk i+1). If discard near zero, surface is dead; pivot to architecture or per-layer GPTQ bit allocation.

hypothesis: Position-weighted TTT loss: linear 0→1 ramp (mean-normalized) on per-token CE so the TTT inner SGD gradient is biased toward deep-context positions that match the sliding-window eval scoring (last 64/2048 positions per window have ~1984 tokens of context), instead of uniformly averaging over all 2048 positions including shallow-context ones whose distribution differs from the eval task.
notes: Untouched surface vs all 10 children of exp_004 — those changed the TTT inner-loop optimizer (Nesterov, layer-LR, WD, momentum reset, freeze-QKV, gate split, temp scale), z-loss, EMA, and GPTQ damp. None reshaped the per-token loss weighting. Loss magnitude preserved (mean weight = 1) so effective LR/grad-clip thresholds carry over from exp_004 unchanged. Cost: ~one extra elementwise mul per TTT step, negligible vs the cross-entropy itself. Risk: if linear ramp is too aggressive, gradient noise on the (smaller effective sample of) deep-context positions could outweigh the eval-alignment gain — in which case a quadratic or step-function variant on positions [1024,2047] is the natural followup.

hypothesis: Double ttt_chunk_tokens 32768 → 65536: halves SGD-step count and doubles per-step batch (LaCT-aligned), reducing TTT gradient noise and freeing all_reduce overhead while preserving cross-chunk momentum continuity (the dominant mechanism that exp_011 showed is +0.015 nats critical).
notes: Untouched surface vs all 11 children of exp_004 — those all touched per-step TTT update rule (Nesterov, layer-LR, WD, momentum reset, freeze-QKV, gate split) or the eval/loss pipeline (temperature, position weighting, z-loss, EMA). This change is orthogonal: it changes adaptation cadence/batch size, not the per-step rule. LaCT (arxiv 2505.23884) argues large chunks (2K-1M) outperform small ones because per-step batches are larger and GPU underutilization is reduced. Counter-risk: half as many adaptation events could reduce reactivity to val-distribution shifts, but cosine-LR floor 0.4 means late chunks still carry 0.4× LR so accumulated drift continues; momentum continuity persists across the 152 chunk transitions.

hypothesis: Switch TTT inner optimizer SGD-momentum→Adam (lr=5e-4, betas=(0.9, 0.95), eps=1e-8): per-param 2nd-moment normalization removes the heterogeneous-grad-magnitude problem that 12 SGD-family children of exp_004 left untouched (all near-noise — Nesterov, layer-LR, WD, momentum reset, ndim-split LR, freeze-QKV, pos-weighted loss, doubled chunks). β2=0.95 stabilizes v within ~20 steps for TTT-without-warmup; peak LR scaled 10× down to match SGD's effective step. Cosine floor 0.4 schedule preserved.
notes: Edit radius: qualitative inner-optimizer family swap (SGD→Adam), not a coefficient tweak. Library prior: PRs #1318, #771, #807, #1647 all use Adam-family for TTT in similar 1.0-1.07 BPB regimes. Risk: Adam's effective step is ~10× SGD-momentum's at same nominal LR, so peak scaled to 5e-4. β2=0.95 (not default 0.999) is critical — TTT has no warmup, default β2 leaves v under-estimated for first ~1000 steps. Counterfactual aligned: from-scratch TTT design today defaults to Adam.

hypothesis: Replace stock Muon Newton-Schulz5 coefficients (3.4445,-4.775,2.0315) with the Polar Express per-iteration minimax tuples (arXiv:2505.16932) — first iteration gets a wide-spectrum kick (a=8.16,b=-22.48,c=15.88) and subsequent four iterations refine; better polar-factor approximation per backend_step → cleaner Muon update directions across ~700 training steps.
notes: Training-side change on a surface untouched by all 13 children of exp_004 (which targeted TTT inner-loop, GPTQ damp, EMA, z-loss, eval temp). Coefficients verified in this environment by PR #1787 (val_bpb 1.0634 with PE+other techniques stacked, +0.0087 nats baseline). Single-axis attribution unknown but PR judge tags Polar NS as "low-medium risk". Numerical sanity: with Frobenius-normalized X, X@X.T entries are O(1/N) scale so b·X@X.T ≈ O(0.025) — within bf16 dynamic range. for-loop unrolls cleanly under torch.compile since steps=5 is fixed.

hypothesis: Freeze depth-recurrence loop bank (blocks 3-5) during TTT in addition to tok_emb — they get 3x training gradient signal (called 3x per forward) and per-chunk SGD on them perturbs all three use-positions simultaneously; SLOT-style freeze-the-bulk transposed onto recurrence topology.</hypothesis> </invoke> -0.001 discard 1.079424 +0.000459 15993442 600.026000 525.400000 1431.000000 apg1-gene-0019

Linear ramp ttt_epochs 2→4 across chunks (rounded, total step count preserved) — reallocate inner-loop adaptation budget toward late chunks where the well-adapted starting state makes each SGD step more informative; continues exp_004's directional thesis (more late-chunk adaptation) by varying step count rather than step magnitude.
notes: Untouched surface vs all 15 discarded children of exp_004. Avg epochs = h.ttt_epochs (3) by construction, so cost-neutral wall-clock. _chunk_epochs = max(1, h.ttt_epochs - 1 + round((ci/(N-1)) * 2)) → {2,3,4} buckets. Cosine LR schedule from exp_004 left unchanged so this is a clean single-axis ablation of step-count distribution. If late-chunk adaptation is undersaturated, gain ≈ -0.0003-0.0005; if late-chunk over-adaptation is the limit, regression ≤ +0.0005. Discriminative either way.

hypothesis: Soft L2 anchor in TTT toward post-train W_0 (alpha=1e-3 per step, applied after each SGD step on non-tok_emb params): caps cumulative drift across ~75 TTT steps without resetting momentum (exp_011's failure) and without decaying toward 0 (exp_007's wrong target).
notes: Untouched surface vs all 16 children of exp_004 — none anchor weights toward post-train state. Standard EWC-style continual-learning regularizer; preserves momentum (exp_011 reset that and lost +0.016) and uses W_0 (post-train) as the decay target rather than 0 (exp_007 used 0 and lost +0.000152). Drift retention 0.999^75 ~ 0.93 — mild but measurable. Memory cost ~10 MB (clone of non-tok_emb params); time cost negligible (one mul+add per param per step).

hypothesis: Soft L2 anchor in TTT toward post-train W_0, applied per-chunk (alpha=3e-3 once after each chunk's inner-epoch loop) — re-test of exp_021 with the lerp moved out of the per-step inner loop to eliminate ~10s eval overhead that DQ'd it; mathematically equivalent drift retention (0.997^24 ~ 0.93).
notes: exp_021 (per-step alpha=1e-3) eval-overran by 4.6s with truncated val_bpb=1.097 — uninformative on whether the anchor itself helps. This re-tests the same hypothesis at chunk granularity: ~24 anchor applications total (vs 75 × 8 ranks) → negligible overhead. Drift retention 0.997^24 = 0.928 matches the original 0.999^75 = 0.928 — same anchor strength, cheaper compute. Distinct from all 16 prior children of exp_004 (none anchor weights toward W_0); distinct from exp_007 (decay toward 0, wrong target) and exp_011 (full momentum reset, +0.016 catastrophic). EWC-style continual-learning regularizer scoped to TTT only.

hypothesis: Lower matrix_clip_sigmas from 12.85 → 8.5 (untouched axis): GPTQ SDClip int6 grid covers ±8.5σ instead of ±12.85σ → 1.51× finer step size → ~2.3× lower quantization noise variance for typical-magnitude weights, while saturation at 8.5σ remains rare for trained transformer weights.
notes: Fully untouched surface vs all 18 children of exp_004 (TTT inner-loop dominated; exp_008 tweaked Hessian damp + block_size, never SDClip sigmas). Mechanism: at sigmas=12.85 the int6 grid wastes resolution on a ±12.85σ range that captures essentially zero mass (transformer weights post-training rarely exceed 4-5σ); at sigmas=8.5 the grid step shrinks 1.51× while saturation at 8.5σ still affects <0.0001% of values. Quantization noise variance ∝ step² so per-weight rounding error variance drops by (1/1.51)² ≈ 0.44. Risk: tighter sigmas widen the int6 distribution → less brotli-compressible → larger blob; with ~0-5KB headroom on the 16MB cap, may size_block. If so, falls back via LOWBIT_LAYERS env in next iteration.

hypothesis: Per-tensor sigma override: apply tight matrix_clip_sigmas=8.5 to attention output projections (.attn.proj.weight, ~12% of model bytes — highest per-byte residual-stream sensitivity) while keeping all other matrices at 12.85, plus loosen embed_clip_sigmas 20→30 to reclaim artifact budget — concentrates exp_023's -0.0043 signal on the most quant-noise-sensitive layers at ~0.18 MB inflation, offset by ~0.3-0.8 MB embed compression savings.
notes: exp_023 (sigma=8.5 uniform) achieved val_bpb=1.0747 (Δ=-0.0043) but blob hit 18.29 MB, 2.29 MB over cap. Mechanism is real; only size budget killed it. Surgical hypothesis: attn.proj writes directly to residual stream — its quant noise propagates through all downstream blocks (compounding effect), so it's the single most byte-efficient place to spend precision. Other matrices (Q/K/V inputs, MLP fc/proj inside their nonlinear envelope) absorb quant noise via softmax/leaky-relu² nonlinearities. Tightening just .attn.proj (11 layers × 262K params = ~12% of model bytes) inflates ~0.18 MB. Compensate via embed_clip_sigmas 20→30: int8 grid coarsens 1.5×, fewer distinct codes used, brotli compresses the embed blob (~1 MB) more efficiently — should free 300-800 KB. If size fits and val_bpb < 1.0775, capture ~30-50% of full sigma signal. If size_blocks, fall back to narrower attn.proj subset (e.g., blocks 7-10 only). If val_bpb stays flat, the gain is concentrated elsewhere (probably MLP) and we know the sigma-cost map.

hypothesis: Per-tensor SDClip σ allocation guided by super-weights literature (arXiv 2411.07191): tighten σ to 8.5 ONLY on .mlp.fc.weight (input proj, fanin=512, no super-weights), revert attn.proj σ to 12.85 (exp_024 confirmed residual-writer outliers must be preserved), loosen embed σ 30→50 to fund the compression bloat under the 16 MB cap.
notes: Targeted refinement of exp_023's mechanism. exp_023 (uniform σ=8.5) achieved val_bpb=1.0747 (-0.0043) but +2.3 MB blob → size_blocked. exp_024 (only attn.proj σ=8.5) was +0.0009 → confirms residual-writers (.attn.proj, by literature analogy also .mlp.proj) carry critical outliers tight clipping destroys. Untouched surface: .mlp.fc.weight (input projections, ~38% of matrix params, fanin=512 narrow distribution, no super-weights per arXiv:2411.07191). Compression compensation via embed σ 30→50 (further loosen, embeds dense within ±2σ). If size_blocked, the bytes returned give a per-tensor calibration point for the next iteration.

hypothesis: Set TTT inner-loop batch_seqs=1 (decoupled from scoring batch_seqs=32) so each chunk runs ~6 SGD steps (1 per minibatch × 2 minibatches × 3 epochs) instead of ~3 — doubles per-chunk adaptation drift via finer per-step granularity, extending exp_004's late-chunk-adaptation mechanism.
notes: Untouched axis vs all 21 children of exp_004 (which targeted TTT optimizer family/per-step scaling/freezing, GPTQ damp, EMA, eval temp, sigma — never the inner-batch granularity). Mechanism: with chunk_seqs=16, world_size=8, my_chunk_seqs=2, current batch_seqs=32 collapses inner loop to 1 step/epoch. Setting inner_batch_seqs=1 → 2 steps/epoch (6 inner SGD steps/chunk vs current 3). Total forward-backward compute is unchanged; only SGD trajectory shifts toward more frequent, smaller-batch updates (8-rank-averaged 8-seq batches vs 16-seq batches). Momentum=0.9 absorbs the added per-step noise (effective averaging window ~10 steps). Eval cost: +10-15s for 2x all_reduce/optimizer.step calls per chunk. exp_016 (opposite direction: bigger chunks → fewer SGD steps) was discarded, providing one-sided prior that the smaller-step direction is preferred. Risk: per-chunk drift doubles → potential over-adaptation to chunk-local distribution; cosine LR floor=0.4 across chunks should still damp.

hypothesis: Pair inner_batch_seqs=1 (from exp_026) with ttt_epochs 3→2: total 4 inner SGD steps/chunk (+33% vs exp_004's 3) at smaller-batch granularity, while reclaiming ~57s eval to stay under the 600s cap that DQ'd exp_026.
notes: Direct refinement of exp_026 (eval_budget_overrun by 3.4s with same inner_batch_seqs=1). The mechanism (more SGD steps via finer minibatch granularity) is untested because exp_026's eval was truncated; ttt_epochs 3→2 trades one epoch of adaptation for ~57s wall-time headroom. Net step count vs exp_004: 4 vs 3 (+33%); vs exp_026: 4 vs 6 (-33%). Per-step batch composition: 8 sequences (8-rank all-reduce of 1 seq each), noisier than exp_004's 16-seq batches but well within momentum=0.9 averaging window. Risk: if the 6-step trajectory in exp_026 was actually beneficial pre-truncation, halving to 4 may underadapt; conversely if 6 over-adapted, 4 should be net positive vs exp_004. Either outcome informs whether more SGD steps via finer batches is the right direction.

hypothesis: Tighten GPTQ SDClip σ from 12.85 → 8.5 on attention Q/K/V input projections (c_q, c_k, c_v) only — finer int6 grid where super-weights are absent (per Apple arXiv:2411.07191: super-weights live in mlp.proj, not QKV); localizes exp_023's proven -0.0043 mechanism to the smallest, safest substrate while staying within budget
notes: exp_023 (uniform σ=8.5) → val_bpb=1.0747 (-0.0043) but +2.3 MB blob (size_blocked). exp_024 (σ=8.5 only on attn.proj) → +0.000931 (within ±0.001 noise, inconclusive). exp_025 (σ=8.5 only on mlp.fc) → size_blocked. Untested partition: c_q+c_k+c_v together (~20% of model bytes; size cost projected ~400-500 KB so size_blocked is a real risk). V has no RMSNorm buffer downstream so it's the most quant-sensitive of the three; Q/K go through RMSNorm and are buffered. If size_blocked, follow-up reduces to c_v alone.

hypothesis: Shuffle TTT inner-loop batch order each of the 3 epochs (chunk-seeded RNG) so SGD updates see decorrelated, closer-to-IID samples instead of three sequential scans through highly auto-correlated within-chunk text — same step count, lower gradient-noise variance.
notes: Untouched axis vs all 24 children of exp_004 — none touched batch ORDER in the TTT inner loop. Mechanism: chronological scan couples adjacent SGD steps to overlapping documents (within-chunk text is contiguous val tokens). Per-epoch reshuffle decorrelates; standard SGD theory predicts lower variance over the 3-epoch average. Chunk-seeded RNG (rank-consistent) keeps DDP all_reduce semantics: every rank reorders the same logical step, so the effective per-step batch is still world_size×batch_seqs of one logical position. Cost: zero extra compute (same step count), trivial Python overhead. Worst case neutral.

hypothesis: Move enable_looping_at 0.35→0.25 — depth-recurrent loop bank (blocks 3-5, invoked 3× per forward pass) activates 10% earlier in training, raising recurrent-mode training fraction from 65%→75% so blocks 3-5 specialize longer for the 3×-invocation regime they perform at eval.
notes: Untouched axis: zero of 25 children of exp_004 touched the loop-activation timing. Web confirmed (2025-2026) layer-importance-aware mechanisms (LieQ, BAQ) for recurrent/shared-weight layers are well-grounded. Tradeoff at 0.25: looping activates at peak LR (warmdown starts at frac=0.28), so 3× gradient amplification shock is at maximum LR. If destabilizing, signal points to needing post-activation LR cooldown. Counterfactual check: step toward "recurrent layers should spend most of training in their final regime" — current 65% leaves specialization on the table.

hypothesis: QuaRot-style Hadamard rotation of every non-embed weight's input axis before GPTQ (W' = W @ H, transformed Hessian H' = H_R^T @ H_orig @ H_R, un-rotated at dequant via deq @ H since normalized Sylvester Hadamard is symmetric-orthogonal): the orthogonal rotation maps each weight row to a near-Gaussian distribution (kurtosis ~3) by spreading outlier energy across input dims, redistributing the GPTQ column-correction bias away from input-aligned directions; row_std preserved so the int6 grid step is unchanged.

Math: GPTQ on (W·H, H_R^T·H_orig·H_R) minimizes ||(W'-W'_q)·x'||² with x'=H_R^T·x; dequant W_q = W'_q·H makes this equal to the original ||(W-W_q)·x||² objective. Compression risk: rotated codes are less peaked at 0 → might increase brotli blob ~100-500KB. Size headroom is ~0KB so a size_blocked verdict is plausible; even then the val_bpb signal tells us if rotation helps quality at all (informative either way).

hypothesis: Async CPU batch prefetch: move mmap data loading to a background thread (queue.Queue maxsize=3) so CPU batch-building overlaps with GPU computation — recovers ~3-5ms/step × ~3000 steps ≈ 10-15 extra training seconds → ~100-150 more gradient steps in the warmdown tail.
notes: No model/architecture change. Worker thread owns all loader state (rng, start_inds) so no locking needed. Main thread only calls queue.get() + non_blocking GPU transfer. hypothesis: ResFormer value residual (arxiv 2410.17897): thread block[0]'s V projection of x0 to all attention layers as a learnable residual (sigmoid-gated, init≈0.047), providing a direct embedding→V shortcut that alleviates attention concentration with zero new weight matrices and 11 tiny scalar parameters.
notes: ResFormer (Ye et al. ACL 2025, arxiv 2410.17897) shows value residual from the first layer added to all subsequent layers achieves equivalent validation loss with 16% fewer parameters. Implementation: compute v_res = blocks[0].attn.c_v(RMSNorm(x0)) once in forward_logits, pass to all blocks; each block's V is augmented by sigmoid(v_res_scale)*v_res where v_res_scale is initialized to -3.0 (sigmoid≈0.047, near-transparent at init). No new weight matrices added; reuses blocks[0].attn.c_v. The 11 per-block v_res_scale scalars go to scalar_params (AdamW lr=0.02). The v_res_scale is adapted during TTT (it's in ttt_params). Expected: improved information flow from embeddings through all attention layers → lower BPB.

hypothesis: Focal loss (γ=2) during training: re-weight per-token CE by (1-pt)²/mean((1-pt)²) where pt=exp(-ce_token), computed via .detach() to avoid gradient through weights. Hard/uncertain tokens get up to ~80× more gradient than easy tokens. TTT adaptation uses standard CE (matching eval objective) via forward_logits + explicit CE. No new parameters; zero memory overhead beyond the detached per-token CE.
notes: Focal loss focuses gradient on uncertain (hard) tokens which have the most room for improvement in an undertrained 600s model. exp(-ce) trick computes pt without a full V-size log_softmax pass. TTT loop changed to use forward_logits + explicit CE so adaptation aligns with the standard CE evaluation metric. Note: z-loss (exp_009) failed badly but that was logit regularization; focal loss is gradient redistribution, a fundamentally different mechanism.

hypothesis: Key residual from block[0] alongside value residual: thread k_res = blocks[0].c_k(attn_norm(x0)*scale) to all attention layers, adding sigmoid(k_res_scale)*k_res to each layer's K before rms_norm. Extends ResFormer to also provide a direct x0→K shortcut, biasing attention patterns toward the initial embedding's key features. 11 new scalar parameters (k_res_scale per block), same forward-pass cost as v_res.
notes: v_res (exp_081, -0.001067) provides direct x0→V shortcut. k_res provides the complementary x0→K shortcut, influencing WHAT the model attends to rather than WHAT it extracts. Both are needed from block[0] because resid_mix[1] is zero-initialized so x0 does not initially reach either V or K through normal flow. k_res is added before rms_norm(k) so it shifts the key direction (rather than being absorbed by normalization as a pure scale).

hypothesis: FreqGPTQ: boost top-100 most-frequent tokens' activations by sqrt(2) during GPTQ Hessian accumulation → 2× weight in H=X^TX for those tokens, biasing int6 quantization fidelity toward positions that dominate val_bpb at zero artifact cost (technique from PR #1721).
notes: Two-pass Hessian collection: pre-collect 64 training batches, compute token counts, identify top-100 by frequency, build freq_weights (1.0 default, sqrt(2)=1.414 for top-100), then replay same batches through hooks with per-position activation scaling. tok_emb output hook remains unweighted. Expected mechanism: frequent tokens cover ~30-50% of BPB-weighted positions; 2× Hessian weight there means GPTQ prioritizes their accuracy over rare tokens.

hypothesis: K residual: thread block[0]'s K projection (computed on x0) to all attention layers as a learnable residual (sigmoid-gated, init≈0.047), analogous to the V residual (exp_081: -0.001067 BPB). Anchors each layer's key representation to the original token embedding, preventing key drift in deep networks and providing direct embedding→K shortcut.
notes: Symmetric to the existing ResFormer V residual. k_res = blocks[0].attn.c_k(_x0_vn) is computed once and passed to all blocks as k_res kwarg. Inside CausalSelfAttention.forward: k = k + sigmoid(k_res_scale)*k_res.reshape(...) before rms_norm(k). 11 new scalar k_res_scale params initialized to -3.0 (sigmoid≈0.047). The ResFormer paper tested V, K, Q residuals and found V best; K may provide complementary benefit on top of existing V residual by stabilizing attention key matching across layers.

hypothesis: Polyak TTT weight averaging (decay=0.998): before scoring each chunk (ci>0), swap in float32 EMA of all prior adapted model weights; after scoring, restore current weights for adaptation; update EMA after each chunk's SGD steps. EMA smooths out chunk-specific overfitting in adapted weights so each chunk is scored with a more globally-representative model state.
notes: EMA decay=0.998 → half-life ~346 chunks; with 305 total chunks this gives a long-memory average weighted toward all past adapted states. Infrastructure failure (internal sync rc=11) blocked exp_141/142 which had this identical change; the failure was infra not code. Swap-in/restore uses in-place p.data.copy_ which is transparent to torch.compile compiled graph. EMA update is inside 'if not is_last_chunk' block (12 spaces) so it only runs when adaptation happened.

hypothesis: Increase TTT SGD momentum from 0.9 to 0.95: strengthens cross-chunk momentum carryover (the mechanism behind exp_004/047/048's monotonic TTT gains), extending the effective adaptation memory from ~10-20 chunks to ~30-50 chunks with 3 SGD steps/chunk. With 305 total val chunks, higher momentum accumulates stronger directional drift toward the val distribution under the constant-LR regime established by exp_048.
notes: Single clean change from exp_147 (rebased to remove Polyak EMA noise from workdir). The momentum buffer persists across all chunks (optimizer not reset between chunks), so higher momentum = more accumulated adaptation velocity. The monotonic TTT axis (LR floor 0→0.4→0.7→1.0 all helped) suggests more cumulative adaptation is beneficial; momentum is the last unexplored dial on this axis. ttt_lr stays at 0.005, ttt_epochs at 3, per-layer scaling 0.5-1.5 unchanged.

hypothesis: Differential attention (DIFF Transformer style, arxiv 2410.05258): split Q/K/V each in half along head_dim (64→32+32), two FA3 calls per layer, subtract per-head learnable λ=sigmoid(diff_lambda, init=-3.0≈0.047) from first attention output; y=cat([y1-λ*y2, y2], dim=-1). Noise cancellation via differential attention maps; no parameter or artifact size increase beyond 11×8=88 trivial scalars.
notes: exp_099 previously attempted DIFF Transformer but had a preflight crash (code bug). This correctly implements it with: (1) .contiguous() on split slices to match FA3's stride requirements, (2) same V split so FA3 gets matching head_dim=32 for all Q/K/V, (3) recombined as cat([y1-λ*y2, y2], -1) to preserve full head_dim=64 for existing proj. XSA still uses full v (not split), attn_out_gate and v_res_scale unchanged. At init λ≈0.047 so model starts near-vanilla and learns differential structure during training.

hypothesis: Replace fixed leaky_relu(h, 0.5).square() with learnable per-layer activation slope: torch.where(h >= 0, h, sigmoid(act_slope) * h).square() where act_slope is a new nn.Parameter(0.0) per MLP block, allowing each layer to adapt its negative slope (sigmoid(0)=0.5=current default) toward relu² (slope→0) or away from it during training.
notes: Learnable activation slopes per MLP layer. Research (arXiv 2208.14111, PR #807) shows learnable slopes tend to converge toward 0 (relu²). 11 new scalar parameters (one per MLP block), negligible artifact impact. Sigmoid parameterization keeps slope in (0,1) and starts at 0.5=current default. act_slope params included in TTT params so they adapt per-chunk at eval time.

hypothesis: Revert harmful learnable MLP activation slope (exp_153 degraded +0.00136 BPB) and add z-loss regularization (1e-4*logsumexp(logits)^2) to training+TTT: z-loss constrains log-partition function scale, complementing existing logit_softcap, preventing overconfident predictions, and potentially improving GPTQ quantization via smaller logit outliers.
notes: Two changes: (1) MLP reverts act_slope (nn.Parameter, learnable slope) back to fixed F.leaky_relu(negative_slope=0.5).square() — exp_153 tried learnable slope and got 1.077379 vs best 1.076019, clear harm. (2) GPT.forward adds z_loss = 1e-4 * logsumexp(flat_logits, dim=-1).square().mean() added to CE loss. z-loss applies during both training and TTT adaptation (model.training=True in both cases). Used in PaLM/T5/Mixtral for router stability; here applied to token logits to bound the partition function. No new parameters — expected to pass size gate cleanly.

hypothesis: Bifurcated q_gain [H,2]: separate learnable temperature scales for RoPE dims (position-aware, first 16 of 64) vs NoPE dims (content-aware, remaining 48), allowing the model to independently optimize attention sharpness in each subspace. Initialization: both columns = 5.25 (same as current single q_gain).
notes: Changes q_gain from shape [H] to [H,2]. In forward: if rope_dims>0 and rope_dims<head_dim, apply g[:,0] to q[...,:rope_dims] and g[:,1] to q[...,rope_dims:], else fallback to g[:,0]. The [H,2] shape is still a control tensor (matches q_gain in CONTROL_TENSOR_NAME_PATTERNS) and goes into AdamW scalar_params. Size delta: 8 extra float32 per attention head × 11 layers = 88 float32 = 352 bytes, stored as float16 passthrough (16 elements ≤ 65536) = 176 bytes in artifact. Negligible size impact. This feature was listed in the seed-stack description as intended but crashed in exp_135/136 due to code bugs; now correctly implemented.

hypothesis: K residual: thread block[0]'s K projection of x0 to all attention layers as a learnable additive residual (sigmoid-gated, init≈0.047), applied after K's RMSNorm — symmetric to V residual (exp_081, -0.001067 BPB). Provides embedding→K shortcuts so each layer's keys carry token-identity signal for induction-head patterns.
notes: V residual (exp_081) gave -0.001067 BPB by threading x0 through block[0].c_v to all layers' V. K residual is symmetric: k_res = blocks[0].attn.c_k(_x0_vn), added after K = RMSNorm(K). Adding after normalization (unlike V which is pre-norm) means k_res acts as a direct additive signal to normalized keys, allowing the model to bias key directions toward token-identity patterns. 11 new k_res_scale scalars (init=-3.0), one per block. Expected smaller gain than V residual because K is consumed via dot-product while V directly influences output — estimate -0.0003 to -0.0005 BPB.

hypothesis: Increase EMA decay 0.9965→0.9985: broader SWA-like averaging over ~1400 steps of the warmdown trajectory instead of the last ~600 steps, smoothing the final checkpoint over more of the convergence basin.
notes: Current EMA (decay=0.9965) has half-life ~197 steps, so the final model mostly reflects the last ~600 steps (all late warmdown). Increasing to 0.9985 gives half-life ~462 steps, covering ~1400 steps of the warmdown (from roughly the midpoint of warmdown through convergence). This is analogous to SWA averaging: Izmailov et al. 2018 showed averaging multiple checkpoints from the 'loss basin' improves generalization. The warmdown phase should represent a traversal of the loss basin; averaging over more of it should give a better minima than just the final noisy checkpoint. No parameter changes, no size impact.

hypothesis: Per-KV-head V-residual gate: expand v_res_scale from shape (1,) to (num_kv_heads=4,) so each KV head independently learns its own embedding-to-V gate strength, symmetric with the existing per-head attn_out_gate and bifurcated q_gain design.
notes: V-residual gate was a single global scalar shared across all 4 KV heads. Now each head has its own sigmoid gate (init=-3.0 → 0.047, near-transparent). This allows heads specialized for copying/local context to use more v_res while heads doing long-range attention suppress it. Adds only 44 float32 scalars total (4 heads × 11 blocks = 88 bytes after float16 passthrough), well within size budget.

hypothesis: Bifurcated K-gain [Hkv,2]: separate learnable temperature scales for RoPE dims (positional) vs NoPE dims (content) on key vectors, symmetric to the existing bifurcated q_gain [H,2]. Init 1.0 (neutral). Each of 4 KV heads independently tunes its RoPE/NoPE key sensitivity, giving full QK symmetry in positional vs content subspace control. k_gain added to CONTROL_TENSOR_NAME_PATTERNS (AdamW, not Muon NS). 8 scalar params per block × 11 blocks = 88 total params (trivial size).
notes: q_gain bifurcation (exp_156) gave -0.000476 by allowing different Q scaling in RoPE vs NoPE dims. K currently has no such bifurcation — K is RMS-normed and RoPE-applied but both dim-groups treated identically. The attention logit q·k is symmetric in Q and K, so per-KV-head K gain in each subspace provides a genuinely new degree of freedom (different from q_gain, since GQA means k_gain[0] controls both q_gain[0] and q_gain[1]). Expected gain less than q_gain since Q is more active in determining attention patterns, but same mechanism.

hypothesis: Increase rope_dims 16→32: equal 50/50 split of positional (RoPE) vs content (NoPE) attention subspace in each head, matching the bifurcated q_gain design that already treats these subspaces independently (exp_156 gained -0.000476). Zero new parameters.
notes: Rebased to exp_159 (removed harmful k_gain from workdir/exp_160). With head_dim=64, rope_dims=16 gave 25% positional / 75% content attention. Changing to 32 gives a symmetric 50/50 split. The q_gain[H,2] bifurcation (learning different Q scales for RoPE vs NoPE dims) was introduced when rope_dims=16, creating an asymmetric split. With rope_dims=32, both subspaces have equal dimensionality (32 each), potentially allowing better learned balance between position-aware and content-aware attention. Standard architectures (LLaMA) use full RoPE (100%), so 50% is a moderate increase from 25%.

hypothesis: Halve TTT chunk size 32768→16384: 2× more adaptation opportunities across the validation sequence (more frequent per-chunk model updates) while keeping total gradient compute constant (2× chunks × half data per step). More frequent updates should better track local distribution drift in the heterogeneous FineWeb eval set.
notes: With chunk_tokens=32768, world_size=8, seq_len=2048: 16 seqs/chunk, 2 seqs/GPU/epoch, 1 gradient step/GPU/epoch = 3 total per chunk, ~305 chunks. With chunk_tokens=16384: 8 seqs/chunk, 1 seq/GPU/epoch = 3 steps/GPU/chunk, ~610 chunks → 2x total gradient steps. Each step operates on half the data (batch [1,2048] vs [2,2048]), so wall-clock per step is ~halved → same total TTT time. Benefit: 2× more distribution-drift correction opportunities, more responsive momentum buffer (faster decay of old gradient info in step-space).

hypothesis: Full-training QAT via straight-through estimator (STE): simulate per-row int6 SDClip quantization in every CastedLinear forward pass during training (clip_sigma=12.85, clip_range=31), so weights learn to cluster near int6 representable values throughout training rather than only in the final ~200 late-QAT steps; _qat_enabled disabled before GPTQ so calibration/quantization use clean fp32 weights.
notes: Gaps.md Gap 1: full-training STE is unexplored. The ~0.019-0.030 BPB quantization gap in the current model (fp32 ~1.045-1.055 vs GPTQ int6 ~1.075) should shrink by 25-50% if weights are trained to be int6-friendly throughout. STE doesn't change gradients (straight-through = identity in backward), only perturbs the forward pass to simulate quantization. _qat_enabled is a class bool set True before torch.compile and False before GPTQ, avoiding the mid-training flip hazard. Only applies to w.numel()>65536 matrices (matching GPTQ threshold), not embeddings (int8).

hypothesis: Learnable per-Q-head XSA subtraction strength (xsa_alpha [num_heads=8] per block): sigmoid(5.0)≈0.993 at init (near-full XSA, preserving current behavior), lets each head independently tune how much self-projection to subtract — copying/syntactic heads can soften toward 0 while contextual heads stay near 1, adding 88 tiny scalar params with zero size impact.
notes: Symmetric to attn_out_gate (per-head gate on attention output) and v_res_scale (per-KV-head V residual gate), both of which yielded improvements. XSA currently applies fixed 100% subtraction of the self-projection; making it learnable per Q-head allows the model to discover optimal per-head self-exclusion strength. alpha=sigmoid(xsa_alpha) so stays in (0,1). Added to CausalSelfAttention.__init__ as xsa_alpha = nn.Parameter(torch.full((num_heads,), 5.0)); used in _xsa_efficient via reshape (1,1,Hkv,group,1) to broadcast over (B,T,Hkv,group,D).

hypothesis: Reduce matrix_clip_sigmas 12.85→5.0 for GPTQ int6: Muon-orthogonalized weight rows have max element ≈3.5σ (Gaussian theory for d=512 columns), so clip_sigmas=12.85 wastes 72% of the quantization range on non-existent tails, giving unnecessarily coarse 0.415·row_std step sizes. Reducing to 5.0 gives 2.57× finer quantization grid with essentially no actual clipping; GPTQ error-propagation compensates any residual tail weights.
notes: This is a pure hyperparameter change (no new parameters, no code size change). Current step size: 12.85*row_std/31≈0.415*row_std. Proposed: 5.0*row_std/31≈0.161*row_std (2.57× finer). For Muon+WD trained weights, max element per row is ~sqrt(2*log(d))*sigma where d=512 → ~3.5*sigma, so clip_sigmas=5.0 captures 100% of typical Muon weights. GPTQ column ordering (most sensitive first by Hessian diag) ensures tail weights are processed last and any small clipping errors propagate only to less-sensitive columns. Baseline clip_sigmas=12.85 appears to be the original seed value, never ablated.

hypothesis: GPTQ block_size 64→32: halves the within-block error-propagation window per matrix, so each column's residual affects at most 31 subsequent columns instead of 63, reducing error accumulation within blocks. Does NOT change the distribution of quantized integer values (same values, computed more accurately), so compressibility and artifact size are unchanged. More frequent Hessian-error corrections for the same total computation.
notes: Context: exp_165 showed clip_sigma=5.0 gives -0.007 BPB improvement but artifact blows up to 21.26 MB due to less-compressible quantized integers (finer grid = less repetition = worse brotli). block_size change is orthogonal: the same integer values get stored but with more accurate error propagation within each block. For MLP fc (2048 cols): 32 blocks→64 blocks, each block 32 cols. For attn matrices (512 cols): 8 blocks→16 blocks. The Hessian-sorted column ordering means the most sensitive columns are in the earliest blocks; tighter propagation there is most valuable. No artifact size change expected.

hypothesis: Post-GPTQ Hessian-weighted per-row scale refinement: after committing integer Q values, replace the statistics-based scale s=clip_sigma*std/clip_range with the least-squares optimal s_opt=sum(H_diag*Q*W)/sum(H_diag*Q^2) per row, strictly minimizing Hessian-weighted reconstruction error at zero artifact size cost.
notes: Motivated by two-stage GPTQ scale optimization literature (arxiv 2602.02126). For fixed integer Q from GPTQ, the statistics-based scale (computed pre-quantization from row std) is sub-optimal. The Hessian-weighted LS solution minimizes sum_j H_jj*(W_j - Q_j*s)^2 and is guaranteed non-worse than the current scale. At clip_sigma=12.85 (loose clipping), Q values are near-zero-centered so the gain may be modest, but the mechanism is sound. Zero size impact: scales are already stored as float16 per-row, integer representation unchanged.

hypothesis: Byte-weighted training CE: weight each token's loss by its UTF-8 byte length (normalized to mean=1.0) to align training objective with BPB evaluation metric — tokens with more bytes get proportionally more gradient, concentrating optimization on tokens that matter most for val_bpb.
notes: Register persistent float32 buffer token_byte_weight in GPT.__init__ by loading SP tokenizer, computing UTF-8 byte lengths for all 8192 vocab tokens (control/unk/byte tokens get length 1, ▁ prefix stripped before encoding), normalizing to mean=1.0. Then pass weight=self.token_byte_weight to F.cross_entropy in GPT.forward. Scoring in eval_val_ttt uses reduction='none' without weights (correct for BPB). GPTQ calibration uses forward_logits directly (unaffected). Buffer numel=8192 below GPTQ threshold so stored as float16 passthrough (~16KB artifact cost). Gaps.md lists token-frequency-stratified loss as explicitly untried.

hypothesis: Byte-weighted training CE (non-persistent buffer): weight each token's loss by its UTF-8 byte length (normalized to mean=1.0) to align training objective with BPB eval metric; buffer persistent=False so it's recomputed in __init__ at both train and eval deserialization — zero artifact cost.
notes: exp_168 was size_blocked by 7374 bytes because the float16 buffer (~16KB) was stored as a persistent passthrough in the GPTQ artifact. Fix: persistent=False. Buffer is recomputed from the SP tokenizer in GPT.__init__ at both training time and eval deserialization (dequantize_mixed creates a fresh GPT(h) which runs __init__ again, so buffer is available for TTT forward pass). Mechanism: BPB weights tokens by UTF-8 byte length; training CE is unweighted; concentrating gradient on longer-byte tokens closes that training/eval misalignment.

hypothesis: Full-Hessian GPTQ scale refinement: replace diagonal-only per-row scale optimization with the exact s*=(W^T H Q)/(Q^T H Q) solution using the full (off-diagonal) activation Hessian, capturing input correlation structure for more accurate per-row quantization scales. Also removes broken byte-weighted CE code from previous session.
notes: Current workdir inherited byte-weighted CE code from exp_168/169 which was causing val_bpb=1.136. First cleanup removes that. Full-Hessian refinement: for each weight row w and quantized integers q, the optimal scale minimizing (w-qs)^T H (w-qs) is s*=(w^T H q)/(q^T H q). Current code uses only diagonal of H (misses off-diagonal activation correlations). The full H captures how activation dimensions co-vary, giving a more accurate scale that reduces the Hessian-weighted reconstruction error. H_orig saved after damping but before column-permutation so it matches Q_final's original ordering. Memory overhead: one [cols,cols] float32 clone per matrix (max 16MB for MLP proj). Compute overhead: two [rows,cols]@[cols,cols] matrix multiplications per matrix on CPU, ~0.5-2s total.

hypothesis: FreqGPTQ: bias GPTQ Hessian calibration toward high-frequency tokens (top-100 get sqrt(2)× activation boost → 2× H weight) so quantization error is minimized on patterns that dominate BPB; zero artifact size cost.
notes: Pre-scan 4 calibration batches to identify top-100 most frequent vocab tokens. During the actual 64-batch Hessian collection, scale each position's activation by sqrt(2.0) when its input token ID is in the top-100 set. This biases H = X^T X to weight high-frequency token activation patterns 2× more, making GPTQ minimize reconstruction error where it matters most for BPB. The 4-batch pre-scan adds negligible eval time (just bincount on already-loaded CPU tensors, no GPU forward passes). No new parameters, no code size increase.

hypothesis: KV temporal shift mixing: add per-KV-head learnable tanh-bounded scalars (init 0=transparent) that blend each position's K and V with the previous position's K/V, giving lightweight local recurrence in key-value space without quantized parameters.
notes: Inspired by PR #684 (KV shift mixing technique). Added k_shift_mix and v_shift_mix as nn.Parameter of shape (num_kv_heads=4,) initialized to zeros. In forward: after RoPE on K and after v_residual on V, apply k = k + tanh(k_shift_mix) * k_shifted (where k_shifted = cat(zeros, k[:,:-1], dim=1)), same for V. These are 1D scalars (ndim<2) so auto-assigned to scalar optimizer (AdamW); numel=4 per tensor << 65536 so GPTQ treats them as float16 passthrough (no artifact size impact). Mechanism: each attention layer gets a local prev-position signal blended into K/V, analogous to a lightweight 1-step recurrence that helps capture transitions and local patterns. Total new persistent storage: 11 layers × 2 params × 4 elements × 2 bytes = 176 bytes.

hypothesis: V normalization (RMSNorm on V before v_res addition): symmetric with existing QK normalization, making attention value vectors unit-scale before the residual is added; improves proj-layer GPTQ Hessian conditioning (more uniform activation scale → better-conditioned H = X^T X) and aligns with QKV-norm literature showing stability benefits at zero parameter cost.
notes: Add F.rms_norm(v, (v.size(-1),)) after c_v reshape but before v_res gate. Analogous to existing QK norm. The normalized V leads to more uniform-magnitude attention outputs y, which should improve GPTQ quantization of proj matrices (better-conditioned Hessian H = X^T X). No new params, same code structure. Expected improvement from QKV-norm literature showing V norm helps training stability when combined with existing QK norm.

hypothesis: Muon-VS (Variance-Scaled Muon, arxiv 2601.14603): add per-row exponential-average squared-norm tracking (beta2=0.95, the existing but unused muon_beta2 hparam) to Muon, dividing the Nesterov gradient by sqrt(EMA(||row||²)) before NS5 orthogonalization — smoother gradient scaling during training transients claims ~1.36× iteration efficiency over standard Muon.
notes: muon_beta2=0.95 was defined in Hyperparameters since at least exp_000 but never wired into the Muon constructor. Muon-VS tracking adds ~6 lines of code, zero model bytes, zero artifact size overhead. Per-row variance (EMA of squared row-norms) is a smoothed version of MuonEq-R's instantaneous row-norm normalization — at steady state they are equivalent; benefit arises during transitions (loop activation at frac=0.35, warmdown at 0.28). Applied only to 2D matrices (g.ndim==2) to avoid corrupting scalar/1D params. Both Muon-VS and MuonEq-R normalization remain in the pipeline; the second is approximately a no-op after Muon-VS brings row norms to ~1.

hypothesis: Continuous frequency-proportional GPTQ weighting: replace binary top-100 FreqGPTQ (only 1.2% of vocab boosted) with sqrt(freq/mean_freq).clamp(0.707, 1.414) applied to all tokens — tokens with above-average frequency get smooth gradient up to 1.41× activation boost (same max as before), rare tokens are down-weighted to 0.71× instead of 1.0, covering ~20% of vocab vs top-100 while using the same 4 pre-scan calibration batches.
notes: The binary top-100 approach had a hard cutoff — tokens at rank 101 got zero boost despite being nearly as common as rank 100. The continuous scheme eliminates this discontinuity. The down-weighting of rare tokens (0.707× vs current 1.0×) is the key new feature: by reducing their Hessian contribution, we concentrate calibration budget on the tokens that actually dominate BPB. Code shrank by ~840 bytes (3 lines → 2 lines). Zero new parameters, same GPTQ calibration time.

hypothesis: One-sided FreqGPTQ: change clamp(0.5, 2.0) to clamp(1.0, 2.0) so rare tokens never get penalized (weight stays ≥ 1.0) while above-average-frequency tokens still get a smooth boost up to sqrt(2)×, fixing the core failure of exp_175's symmetric scheme that downweighted rare tokens and hurt BPB.
notes: exp_175 (symmetric clamp 0.5-2.0) regressed by +0.002527 vs exp_171 (binary top-100). The penalty on rare tokens was the failure mode: rare tokens have high per-occurrence BPB impact and need accurate GPTQ calibration. One-sided clamping removes the penalty while preserving the high-frequency boost. The continuous boost now covers ~40% of vocab (above-average freq tokens) vs 1.2% binary, but without any downweighting of the remaining 60%.

hypothesis: Enable depth recurrence earlier (enable_looping_at 0.35→0.25): activates the 3-layer loop (layers 3-5 invoked 3×) at 150s instead of 210s, giving ~300 more looped training steps (~60s × ~5 steps/s) with the effectively deeper (~17-layer) architecture, at zero parameter cost.
notes: Looping activation timing hasn't been directly tuned as an isolated experiment. exp_146 bundled warmdown_frac and other changes together but enable_looping_at=0.35 was inherited from the baseline. The warmup code already compiles both the looped and non-looped graphs (warmup_steps=20 runs both), so early activation should not cause compile instability. More looped steps means the depth-recurrence advantage (ResFormer v_res + bifurcated q_gain + XSA all operating within the looped segment) compounds over more gradient updates.

hypothesis: Remove full-Hessian GPTQ scale refinement (s*=(W^T H Q)/(Q^T H Q)): exp_170 showed this feature costs +0.000157 BPB yet it persisted into exp_171's code; removing it restores the simpler per-row SDClip scale that was empirically superior. Also reverts enable_looping_at to 0.35.
notes: Full-Hessian scale refinement was added in exp_170 (BPB 1.075575 vs exp_159 parent 1.075418 = +0.000157 hurt). Despite being demonstrably harmful, the code stayed in the workdir through exp_171. Removing it plus restoring enable_looping_at=0.35 brings workdir to a clean exp_171-equivalent minus the harmful feature. Expected ~1.075227.

hypothesis: Increase GPTQ calibration batches 64→96: 50% more Hessian data gives a more accurate H=X^T X estimate, reducing GPTQ reconstruction error at zero artifact size cost (Q and scale tensors are unchanged in size; only Hessian quality improves).
notes: exp_178 established that removing full-Hessian scale gives BPB=1.075363 (-0.000021 improvement) but artifact_bytes=16000693 (693 bytes over limit). The full-Hessian scale code produces more compressible Q values, so it must remain for size compliance. This experiment instead targets Hessian quality: more calibration batches improves the H estimate used by GPTQ, potentially giving -0.0001 to -0.0003 BPB without any model size change. eval_s was 502s in exp_178 so adding ~10s for 32 more calibration batches is well within the 600s eval budget.

hypothesis: Learnable per-block MLP negative slope (PReLU²): replace fixed leaky_relu(0.5)^2 with a learnable neg_slope parameter (init=0.5) per block, allowing each of 11 blocks to independently discover its optimal negative-input activation strength. At init, activation is identical to current; model learns if 0.5 is optimal per layer.
notes: Motivated by PR #807 "learned activation mixing" technique, parameterized as PReLU² instead of a blend. Different layers may need different negative slopes: early layers process raw token features (may benefit from more linear slope), late layers compose features (may benefit from harder gating). Each neg_slope (11 total scalar params) is stored as float16 passthrough in GPTQ artifact - negligible size. Gradient flows through clamp+multiply which is compile-compatible. TTT will also adapt these scalars per chunk.

hypothesis: Replace MLP activation from leaky_relu(0.5)² to ReLU² (pure squared ReLU, negative_slope=0→0): maximum activation sparsity (~50% zero activations) creates sparser GPTQ Hessians, concentrating calibration budget on non-zero positions for better int6 reconstruction, while ReLU² has been shown to match SwiGLU perplexity in LLMs (arXiv 2402.03804) and benefit quantized inference.
notes: Rebased to clean exp_171 (removing accumulated PReLU² code from failed exp_180). Changed F.leaky_relu(h, negative_slope=0.5).square() to F.relu(h).square() in MLP.forward(). The negative slope 0.5 contributes 0.25x² for negative activations — zeroing this maximizes sparsity. With 2048-dim hidden layer, ~1024 activations become exactly zero per token, making the GPTQ H=X^TX matrix sparser and better conditioned for the error-propagation step. Code bytes decrease from 18696→18645 (shorter expression). Model bytes unchanged in structure but will differ in values due to different activation-driven weight distributions.

hypothesis: Learned per-block MLP activation mixing: add a scalar act_mix=nn.Parameter(tensor(-4.0)) per block so each block independently learns the optimal blend of relu² and leaky_relu(0.5)² via sigmoid(act_mix)*relu(h) + (1-sigmoid(act_mix))*leaky_relu(h,0.5); init at -4.0 → σ≈0.018 so training starts near exp_171's leaky_relu(0.5)² behavior while allowing blocks to learn toward relu² sparsity where it helps.
notes: Current workdir was at exp_181 state (pure relu²). This change replaces pure relu with a learned per-block sigmoid-gated blend starting at ~leaky_relu(0.5)² behavior. Mechanism: different layers may optimally use different sparsity levels — early blocks may stay near leaky_relu² (preserving small activations) while later blocks may shift toward relu² (more sparsity for refined predictions). act_mix is a 0-dim scalar → passthrough in GPTQ (stored as float16, 2 bytes/block). Model bytes identical to exp_171 since weight matrices are unchanged. Code size: +3 lines = ~100 bytes LZMA.

hypothesis: AdamW TTT (betas=(0.9,0.95), lr=0.001): replace SGD with AdamW for per-parameter adaptive learning rates during TTT; accumulated second-moment statistics across chunks act as learned per-parameter importance weights, normalizing updates by gradient variance and potentially improving adaptation quality vs. SGD's uniform momentum.
notes: ttt_lr reduced from 0.005→0.001 (AdamW normalized updates ~= SGD raw LR × momentum boost), betas=(0.9,0.95) reuses existing ttt_momentum. AdamW state carries across chunks like SGD momentum; v_t (second moment) learns which params have high gradient variance → smaller effective LR → prevents overshooting on noisy params. Completely untried in our lineage. Expected eval_s similar or slightly lower than current 575-595s (AdamW step is slightly more expensive than SGD).

hypothesis: Z-loss regularization (alpha=1e-4): add 1e-4 * mean(log(sum(exp(logits)))^2) to training CE; penalizes large log-partition-function to prevent logit magnitude growth, stabilize training, and improve calibration — used in PaLM/Gemini but untried in our lineage.
notes: AdamW TTT (exp_183) failed catastrophically (+0.14 BPB) because gradient normalization makes TTT updates ~1000x larger than SGD raw-gradient updates — SGD is correct for TTT. Pivoting to training-loss regularization. Z-loss prevents logit norms from growing during the 600s training window, which can improve final model calibration and reduce BPB at eval. Zero parameter cost, ~2 FLOP overhead per position. Log-z is already computed in cross_entropy internally, so torch.compile should fuse this efficiently.

hypothesis: LAWA (Latest Weight Averaging): replace EMA with uniform average of 5 explicit model snapshots taken at training fracs 0.85, 0.88, 0.91, 0.94, 0.97 — captures the convergent warmdown tail more precisely than the 286-step-window EMA (arxiv 2306.03241).
notes: EMA with decay=0.9965 gives ~286-step effective window covering the last ~6% of training. LAWA with 5 snapshots at fracs 0.85-0.97 gives a uniform average over the last 15% of training — wider coverage with equal weighting of each convergence point. According to arxiv 2306.03241, LAWA outperforms EMA especially when models are trained with high LRs (we use Muon which can have high effective LR). Parameter-free: snapshots live only on CPU RAM during training, not in the artifact. Falls back to EMA if fewer than 3 snapshots collected.

hypothesis: Last-4-blocks TTT (blocks 7-10 only) with ttt_epochs=5: restrict TTT adaptation to the last 4 blocks (lr_scale 1.2-1.5) and increase epochs 3→5 — faster backward (skip 7/11 blocks, ~60% backward savings) makes 5 epochs fit within the eval budget while focusing adaptation on semantically-rich later layers.
notes: Motivation from arxiv 2404.03784 (GALA): not all layers are equally receptive to TTT adaptation; layers with misaligned gradients can hurt. Our per-layer LR scaling (0.5-1.5) already deprioritizes early blocks. Making this explicit by freezing blocks 0-6 during TTT should: (1) reduce gradient noise from early layers, (2) allow 5 epochs in the same wall-clock budget since backward propagates only through 4/11 blocks (~60% backward speedup → per-epoch ~60% of current → 5 epochs at 5×0.6/3=1.0× current TTT cost). Blocks 7-10 have lr_scale 1.2-1.5, highest in the schedule, and handle document-level semantics most relevant for per-chunk adaptation.

hypothesis: Increase rope_dims from 16 to 24 (37.5% of head_dim=64) to give the model richer positional encoding across more frequency pairs, with no parameter cost since q_gain bifurcation already handles the RoPE/NoPE split dynamically.
notes: Parameter-free change: rope_dims 16→24. The bifurcated q_gain [H,2] applies col-0 gain to the first rope_dims dimensions (RoPE region) and col-1 gain to the remaining NoPE dims, so it adapts automatically. The Rotary module only uses rope_dims dims for sin/cos, passing remaining 40 dims through unchanged. More position-frequency pairs may help capture finer positional distinctions in the 2048-token training window.

hypothesis: FreqGPTQ stronger boost: increase top-100 token activation multiplier from sqrt(2.0) to sqrt(3.0) (2× → 3× H weight), more aggressively minimizing GPTQ reconstruction error on the tokens that dominate BPB while keeping the same narrow top-100 coverage.
notes: exp_171 (sqrt(2)) gave -0.000034; exp_175/176 (broader smooth weighting) gave +0.002. This tries stronger boosting with same narrow top-100 coverage. Distinguishes coverage vs. boost-magnitude as the key dimension. Top-100 tokens account for ~50-60% of corpus occurrences (Zipf). With sqrt(3), they get 3× H weight = 78% of total Hessian contribution (vs 71% with sqrt(2)).

hypothesis: Remove full-Hessian GPTQ scale refinement (s*=(W^T H Q)/(Q^T H Q)): exp_178 confirmed this feature hurts BPB (exp_170 cost +0.000157 BPB); clean rebase to exp_171 then removing only these 10 lines avoids the size_blocked issue that plagued exp_178 due to accumulated workdir code.
notes: exp_178 showed BPB=1.075363 (better than exp_171's 1.075384) but was size_blocked at 16000693 bytes due to accumulated PReLU/activation-mixing code in the workdir at time of submission. This experiment: clean rebase to exp_171 snapshot (85758 bytes), then removes only H_orig=H.clone() and the 10-line scale refinement block, returning the simple SDClip scale s directly. Code shrinks from ~19600 to ~18574 bytes, giving ~1026 bytes of headroom vs exp_171. The full-Hessian refinement computes s* = (W^T H Q)/(Q^T H Q) using the off-diagonal Hessian — this double-dips on calibration data, potentially introducing overfitting artifacts into the scale that simple per-row std gives more robustly.

hypothesis: Focal loss (gamma=0.5) during training: down-weight easy tokens (high predicted probability) and up-weight hard/rare tokens, giving the model richer gradient signal on infrequent vocabulary items that disproportionately inflate BPB.
notes: Implements standard focal loss FL = -(1-p)^gamma * log(p) with gamma=0.5 as the training objective, replacing standard CE. The weight (1-p)^0.5 smoothly down-weights tokens the model already predicts well (p→1 gives weight→0) and up-weights tokens where the model is uncertain (p→0.5 gives weight≈0.71). Applied in GPT.forward() which is called both during training and TTT (so TTT also focuses on hard tokens). Clean rebase to exp_171 snapshot. The mechanism targets Gap 5 from gaps.md (token-frequency-stratified loss): BPE tokens with low corpus frequency are typically harder to predict, so focal loss implicitly addresses rare-token under-prediction. MiLe Loss paper (arxiv 2310.19531) confirms focal-loss-style reweighting gives consistent improvement across 468M-6.7B parameter models. exp_189 showed GPTQ changes are a dead end (inflate model bytes + hurt BPB); this is a clean training-side change.

hypothesis: Deeper LR warmdown: reduce min_lr from 0.05 to 0.01 (decay to 1% of peak instead of 5%), allowing the cosine warmdown's final convergence phase to reach near-zero LR as recommended by WSD / D2Z research.
notes: WSD research (arxiv 2602.06797, ICML 2025) shows 'decay-to-zero' (D2Z) systematically outperforms decay-to-nonzero-minimum for LLM training. Our warmdown_frac=0.72 means 72% of training time is in the cosine decay phase; with min_lr=0.05, the LR bottoms out at 5% of peak (e.g., 0.022×0.05=0.0011 for Muon), leaving the model still updating at a non-trivial rate at the end. With min_lr=0.01, the minimum LR is 0.022×0.01=0.00022 — much closer to zero, allowing tighter final convergence. PR #1787 uses min_lr=0.001 (0.1% of peak) and achieves val_bpb=1.0634 vs our 1.0754. This single-char change tests whether deeper decay helps in our regime. Clean rebase to exp_171 snapshot; no focal_gamma code. exp_189 and exp_190 both size_blocked for different reasons (GPTQ scale change and significant model weight redistribution); a pure LR-schedule change should stay within 16MB based on 11/12 empirical success rate.

hypothesis: Remove full-Hessian GPTQ scale refinement (s*=(W^T H Q)/(Q^T H Q)): exp_178 measured BPB=1.075363 from this edit but was size_blocked due to accumulated workdir code; rebased to clean exp_171 base, code shrinks by ~11 lines so artifact fits.
notes: exp_178 and exp_189 both tried this same edit from bloated workdirs and were size_blocked (16000693 bytes). Clean rebase to exp_171 (85758 bytes base) makes the code 85574 bytes — smaller than 171. exp_170 added the refinement and got +0.000157 BPB worse; exp_178/189 confirmed removal improves BPB. Expected small improvement ~-0.00002 consistent with exp_178 measurement.

hypothesis: ExoFormer K residual (arxiv 2601.08131): add block[0]'s K projection of normalized x0 as a per-KV-head gated residual to all blocks' K, symmetric with the existing successful V residual; gate init sigmoid(-3.0)≈0.047, zero artifact size overhead.
notes: ExoFormer (Su 2025, arxiv 2601.08131) extends ResFormer V-residual to Q/K/V/gates with exogenous anchors, showing that K and Q residuals are beneficial when anchor sources are normalized. Our V residual (exp_081, -0.001067) uses block[0].attn.c_v(attn_norm(x0)) - already normalized source. This adds the symmetric K residual: k_res = blocks[0].attn.c_k(attn_norm(x0)) added before K RMSNorm with sigmoid-gated per-KV-head scale. 44 new scalar params (44×float16=88 bytes). k_res added before K normalization so RMSNorm handles combined direction.

hypothesis: Q-residual anchor (ExoFormer-style): thread block[0]'s Q projection of normalized x0 as a per-head gated residual to all blocks' Q (after RMSNorm, before RoPE+gain), symmetric with the existing V-residual that gave -0.001067 BPB. Init q_res_scale=-3.0 (sigmoid≈0.047, near-transparent). Provides raw-token query-intent anchors across all layers, especially for recurrent blocks 3-5. 88 new scalars → ~176 bytes artifact impact.
notes: V-residual gave -0.001067 (exp_081) but K-residual hurt +0.001182 (exp_193, size_blocked but BPB was valid at 1.076566). Q is the 'query side' like V (both active computations), unlike K which confused matching. q_res = blocks[0].attn.c_q(x0_normed), shape [B,T,512]. Added after RMSNorm on Q so both Q and q_res are in unit-norm space. Gate sigmoid(-3.0)=0.047 at init. Clean rebase from exp_171 (85758 bytes).

hypothesis: MuonTTT: replace SGD+momentum TTT optimizer with Muon-style NS-normalized momentum — accumulate gradient into momentum buffer (same as current), then apply zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 to 2D matrix buffers before the weight update; 1D params use plain heavy-ball; ns_steps=5 matches training quality.
notes: LaCT paper (arXiv 2505.23884) shows Muon consistently outperforms plain SGD+momentum for TTT across chunk sizes. NS normalization gives each 2D weight matrix a calibrated spectral-norm-1 update direction, preventing over/under-updating of different layers. Pairs with existing depth-based lr_scale [0.5,1.5] for relative layer differentiation. Zero parameter overhead (no new model params). Rebased clean from exp_171 before edit — prior workdir had Q-residual code from size_blocked exp_194 which would have contaminated results.

hypothesis: MuonTTT with ttt_epochs=2: NS-normalized momentum TTT (LaCT arXiv 2505.23884) using zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 on 2D momentum buffers; reduce epochs 3→2 to fit eval budget (exp_195 overran by 3s with epochs=3, saving ~45s by dropping one epoch while adding ~18s NS overhead).
notes: exp_195 showed eval budget overrun at 603.1s with MuonTTT+3epochs. NS normalization adds ~18s overhead (all 2D matrices, all 8 GPUs, 45 gradient steps × 5 NS steps). Fix: epochs 3→2 saves ~45s TTT training time, net eval ~558-575s. Trade-off: fewer adaptation steps but each step has better gradient quality via spectral normalization. LaCT paper shows Muon TTT quality is high enough to compensate for fewer steps vs plain SGD+momentum.

hypothesis: MuonTTT with min_dim=64 gate: NS-normalized momentum TTT applied only to large weight matrices (both dims ≥ 64), avoiding recompilation overhead from small control tensors (q_gain 8×2, resid_mix 2×512) that use AdamW in training so their shapes aren't in the NS compile cache; ttt_epochs=3 restored.
notes: Root cause of eval_budget_overrun in exp_195/196: zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 called with shapes (2,512) and (8,2) for resid_mix/q_gain — these control tensors are in scalar_params during training (AdamW, no NS), so their shapes were never compiled. MuonTTT triggered recompilation adding ~8s fixed overhead regardless of epoch count (same 603s in both exp_195 and exp_196). Fix: only NS-normalize tensors where both dims >= 64 (all real weight matrices: c_q 512x512, c_k 256x512, c_v 256x512, proj 512x512, mlp.fc 2048x512, mlp.proj 512x2048 — all in training Muon compile cache). Small tensors use plain heavy-ball momentum. Net eval_s should return to ~585-595s.

hypothesis: MuonTTT ns_steps=2 diagnostic: reduce NS iterations from 5 to 2 to test if NS compute is the source of the 8s eval overhead (if still 603s, overhead is from Python loop iteration, not NS; if ~598s, overhead was NS compute).
notes: Diagnostic experiment. exp_195 (ns_steps=5, 3 epochs), exp_196 (ns_steps=5, 2 epochs), exp_197 (ns_steps=5, min_dim=64, 3 epochs) all gave exactly 603.1s. If the overhead is from NS compute, ns_steps=2 (40% of ns_steps=5) should give 603-8*(1-2/5)=603-4.8=598.2s. If still 603s, overhead is from Python loop iteration (~100 param groups x 45 steps), not NS ops, and MuonTTT must be abandoned.

hypothesis: Increase warmdown_frac from 0.72 to 0.78: spend 78% of the 600s training budget in cosine LR decay (vs current 72%), giving more steps at sub-peak LR for tighter convergence as recommended by WSD/D2Z literature (arxiv 2602.06797).
notes: WSD paper shows longer warmdown fraction consistently improves LLM perplexity. Current 0.72 gives ~168s at peak LR then 432s warmdown. At 0.78: ~132s at peak LR then 468s warmdown. Muon momentum warmup uses 0.22 fraction, so the flat portion is 0.78-0.22=0.56 (down from 0.50 at 0.72). This is a pure hyperparameter change, zero code size impact. The expected mechanism: the cosine warmdown phase allows the optimizer to settle into a sharper local minimum; with 78% warmdown the model benefits from ~36s more of fine-grained convergence.

hypothesis: Increase warmdown_frac from 0.72 to 0.75: spend 75% of training budget in cosine LR decay instead of 72%, giving more convergence time per WSD/D2Z literature while avoiding the 2.7KB size increase seen with warmdown_frac=0.78.
notes: exp_199 showed warmdown_frac=0.78 gives val_bpb=1.075137 (-0.000247 improvement) but size_blocked at 16001562 (2699 bytes over limit). The extra bytes come from different weight convergence patterns that compress worse with brotli. warmdown_frac=0.75 is a smaller step: less training dynamics change = more similar compression to exp_171's 15998863 bytes. Expected BPB improvement proportionally smaller (~-0.00015) but more likely to fit within 16MB. Muon momentum warmup is 0.22 so the constant-LR phase = 0.75-0.22=0.53 (down from 0.56 at 0.72).
